acceptedAnswer.text: Most taxable foreign remittances require them, but a specified list of payments under Rule 37BB is exempt from the 15CA/15CB requirement.

posts.title: TDS on the GST component of an invoice
posts.excerpt: Is TDS deducted on the GST portion of an invoice? Per CBDT Circular 23/2017, TDS is deducted on the amount excluding GST where GST is shown separately on the invoice.
posts.content:
Per CBDT Circular 23/2017, income-tax TDS is deducted on the amount paid or payable excluding the GST component, provided the GST is indicated separately on the invoice. If GST is not shown separately, TDS is deducted on the whole (gross) amount.

The rule

CBDT Circular 23/2017 clarified that where, under the contract or invoice, the GST on services is shown separately, TDS under Chapter XVII-B is deducted on the amount excluding that GST. This avoids deducting tax on tax. The clarification applies across TDS sections (194C, 194J, 194I, etc.).

The condition and a caution

The relief applies only when GST is separately indicated. If the invoice shows a single consolidated amount without splitting GST, TDS is deducted on the gross amount. Note the circular refers to GST on services; for composite supplies, ensure the GST is clearly bifurcated.

Worked example

Professional fee Rs.1,00,000 + GST 18% Rs.18,000 = Rs.1,18,000, with GST shown separately. TDS under 194J at 10% = Rs.10,000 (on Rs.1,00,000), not Rs.11,800. The payee receives Rs.1,08,000 and the GST is paid in full.

Practical points

  • Always split GST on invoices to your customers to enable correct TDS.
  • The same logic applies to TDS under GST (Section 51) on the taxable value.
  • Document the bifurcation for audit.

Frequently asked questions

Is TDS deducted on the GST amount?

No, provided GST is shown separately on the invoice. Per CBDT Circular 23/2017, TDS is deducted on the amount excluding the GST component.

What if GST is not shown separately?

Then TDS is deducted on the gross (GST-inclusive) amount, as the relief in Circular 23/2017 requires GST to be separately indicated.

Does this apply to all TDS sections?

Yes, the principle applies across Chapter XVII-B TDS sections such as 194C, 194J and 194I where GST on services is shown separately.

Key takeaways

  • TDS is on the value excluding separately-shown GST (Circular 23/2017).
  • If GST is not split, TDS is on the gross amount.
  • Applies across 194C/194J/194I and similar sections.
  • Always bifurcate GST on invoices for correct TDS.

faq.name: What if GST is not shown separately?

acceptedAnswer.text: Then TDS is deducted on the gross (GST-inclusive) amount, as the relief in Circular 23/2017 requires GST to be separately indicated.

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, the principle applies across Chapter XVII-B TDS sections such as 194C, 194J and 194I where GST on services is shown separately.

posts.title: TDS on rent: Section 194I vs 194IB
posts.excerpt: TDS on rent - Section 194I (10% land/building, 2% plant) applies to businesses above the threshold, while Section 194IB makes individuals/HUF deduct 2% on rent above Rs.50,000 a month.
posts.content:
Section 194I requires businesses to deduct TDS on rent at 10% for land/building and 2% for plant and machinery, above an annual threshold; Section 194IB requires individuals and HUFs (not under tax audit) to deduct 2% on rent exceeding Rs.50,000 per month.

Section 194I (business tenants)

Rent ofRate
Land or building (incl. furniture)10%
Plant, machinery or equipment2%

194I applies to businesses and professionals liable to audit, once annual rent to a landlord exceeds the threshold (Rs.6,00,000 per year following the Finance Act 2025; Rs.2,40,000 earlier). ✓ Rs.6,00,000 p.a. threshold (194I) per Finance Act 2025; Rs.50,000/month (194IB). Verified June 2026. the current threshold.

Section 194IB (individual/HUF tenants)

194IB targets individuals and HUFs not liable to tax audit who pay rent. They must deduct 2% (reduced from 5% with effect from 1 October 2024) where monthly rent exceeds Rs.50,000. The deduction is made once a year (in the last month of the tenancy or the financial year), not monthly, and is paid using a challan-cum-statement in Form 26QC — no TAN is needed.

Comparison

Feature194I194IB
Who deductsBusiness / audit casesIndividual/HUF (non-audit)
Rate10% / 2%2%
ThresholdRs.6,00,000 p.a. ✓Rs.50,000 per month
TAN requiredYesNo (Form 26QC)
FrequencyMonthlyOnce a year

Practical points

  • Rent for 194I is the amount excluding GST, where GST is shown separately (CBDT Circular 23/2017).
  • 194IB deduction is capped so it does not exceed the last month's rent.
  • Collect the landlord's PAN; without it, 194IB deducts at 20% (capped at last month's rent).
  • Coordinate with GST on rent.

Frequently asked questions

What is the TDS rate on rent under Section 194I?

10% for rent of land or building and 2% for rent of plant, machinery or equipment, above the annual threshold.

Who deducts TDS under Section 194IB?

Individuals and HUFs not liable to tax audit, on rent exceeding Rs.50,000 per month, at 2% (reduced from 5% from 1 October 2024), once a year via Form 26QC.

Is TDS on rent computed on the GST-inclusive amount?

No. Where GST is shown separately on the invoice, TDS under 194I is deducted on the rent excluding GST, per CBDT Circular 23/2017.

Key takeaways

  • 194I (business): 10% land/building, 2% plant; annual threshold.
  • 194IB (individual/HUF): 2% on rent above Rs.50,000/month.
  • 194IB is deducted once a year via Form 26QC (no TAN).
  • TDS on rent excludes separately-shown GST.

acceptedAnswer.text: 10% for rent of land or building and 2% for rent of plant, machinery or equipment, above the annual threshold.

faq.name: Who deducts TDS under Section 194IB?

acceptedAnswer.text: Individuals and HUFs not liable to tax audit, on rent exceeding Rs.50,000 per month, at 2% (reduced from 5% from 1 October 2024), once a year via Form 26QC.

faq.name: Is TDS on rent computed on the GST-inclusive amount?

acceptedAnswer.text: No. Where GST is shown separately on the invoice, TDS under 194I is deducted on the rent excluding GST, per CBDT Circular 23/2017.

posts.title: TDS on salary under the new tax regime
posts.excerpt: How TDS on salary works under Section 192 with the new tax regime as default - the average-rate method, employee declaration, standard deduction, and switching between regimes.
posts.content:
TDS on salary under Section 192 is deducted at the employee's average rate of income tax, computed on the estimated annual salary under the new tax regime by default, unless the employee opts for the old regime. The employer applies the Rs.75,000 standard deduction and divides the annual tax across the months.

The average-rate method

Section 192 does not have a fixed rate. The employer estimates the employee's annual taxable salary, computes the tax on it (under the chosen regime), and deducts 1/12th of that annual tax each month (the average rate). As salary or declarations change, the monthly TDS is re-balanced.

New regime as default

Since the new regime is the default, the employer computes salary TDS under it unless the employee specifically intimates a choice of the old regime. Under the new regime, the employer allows the Rs.75,000 standard deduction and the employer's NPS contribution under 80CCD(2), but not 80C, HRA, LTA or home-loan interest. See old vs new regime.

Employee declarations

  • The employee declares the chosen regime; absent a declaration, the new regime applies.
  • For the old regime, the employee submits proof of deductions (80C, HRA rent receipts, home-loan interest) for the employer to consider.
  • Other income and TDS can be reported to the employer in Form 12BB.

Compliance

  • Deposit salary TDS by the 7th of the next month (April for March).
  • File the quarterly Form 24Q; issue Form 16 by 15 June.
  • Under the new Act 2025, salary TDS moves to Section 392.
  • Compute the employee's tax with the Income Tax Calculator.

Frequently asked questions

How is TDS on salary calculated?

The employer estimates annual taxable salary, computes the income tax on it under the applicable regime, and deducts one-twelfth of that tax each month (the average rate).

Which regime does the employer use for salary TDS?

The new regime by default; the old regime is used only if the employee specifically opts for it and provides proof of the relevant deductions.

Is the standard deduction available for salary TDS?

Yes — Rs.75,000 under the new regime and Rs.50,000 under the old regime, applied by the employer when computing TDS.

Key takeaways

  • Section 192: TDS at the average rate on estimated annual salary.
  • New regime is the default; old regime only on the employee's option.
  • Standard deduction Rs.75,000 (new) / Rs.50,000 (old).
  • File Form 24Q; issue Form 16 by 15 June; new-Act Section 392.

posts.title: TDS on property purchase under Section 194IA
posts.excerpt: Buying immovable property of Rs.50 lakh or more? Section 194IA requires the buyer to deduct 1% TDS and pay it via Form 26QB. The rules, joint-buyer position, and no-TAN process.
posts.content:
Section 194IA requires the buyer of immovable property (other than agricultural land) to deduct TDS at 1% where the consideration or the stamp-duty value is Rs.50 lakh or more. The buyer pays it using Form 26QB within 30 days, without needing a TAN.

The rule

On purchase of immovable property (land or building, other than rural agricultural land) where the consideration or stamp-duty value is Rs.50 lakh or more, the buyer deducts 1% TDS on the higher of the two values, at the time of payment or credit. No TAN is required; the buyer uses Form 26QB (a challan-cum-statement) and issues Form 16B to the seller.

Joint buyers and sellers

Where there are multiple buyers or sellers, the Rs.50 lakh threshold is judged on the total consideration of the property, not each person's share. Each buyer files a 26QB for their portion. For a non-resident seller, TDS is governed by Section 195 (not 194IA), usually at a higher rate.

Process

  1. Deduct 1% at each payment/instalment.
  2. File Form 26QB within 30 days of the end of the month of deduction.
  3. Download Form 16B and give it to the seller.
  4. Ensure the seller's PAN is correct (else 20% under 206AA).

Common errors

  • Not deducting because each instalment is below Rs.50 lakh — the threshold is the property value, not the instalment.
  • Deducting 1% when the seller is a non-resident — that case is Section 195.
  • Missing the stamp-value comparison.
  • Late 26QB — attracts 234E fee and 201(1A) interest.

Frequently asked questions

When is TDS deducted on buying property?

When the consideration or stamp-duty value of the immovable property (other than rural agricultural land) is Rs.50 lakh or more; the buyer deducts 1% under Section 194IA.

Do I need a TAN for 194IA TDS?

No. The buyer uses Form 26QB, a challan-cum-statement, and does not need a TAN; Form 16B is then issued to the seller.

What if the seller is a non-resident?

Section 194IA does not apply; TDS is governed by Section 195, generally at a higher rate based on the capital gains, with treaty relief where applicable.

Key takeaways

  • 194IA: 1% TDS on property of Rs.50 lakh or more.
  • Threshold is the property value, not the instalment.
  • Pay via Form 26QB within 30 days; no TAN needed.
  • Non-resident seller: use Section 195, not 194IA.

posts.title: TDS rate chart FY 2026-27: a quick guide
posts.excerpt: A practical guide to the TDS rate chart for FY 2026-27 - common sections (194C, 194J, 194I, 194H, 194A), rates, thresholds, and the new Income-tax Act 2025 section mapping.
posts.content:
TDS rates for FY 2026-27 range from 0.1% to 30% depending on the nature of payment, with common rates of 1-2% for contractors (194C), 10% for professional fees (194J) and rent of property (194I), and 2% for commission (194H). Higher rates apply where the payee has no PAN (Section 206AA) or is a non-filer (206AB).

Common TDS sections at a glance

SectionPaymentRateThreshold (FY 2026-27)
192SalarySlab (average rate)Basic exemption
194AInterest (other than securities)10%Rs.50,000 (bank) / Rs.1,00,000 (senior citizen) — Finance Act 2025 ✓
194CContractor1% / 2%Rs.30,000 single / Rs.1,00,000 p.a.
194HCommission / brokerage2%Rs.20,000 — Finance Act 2025 ✓
194IRent10% / 2%Rs.6,00,000 p.a. — Finance Act 2025 ✓
194JProfessional / technical fees10% / 2%Rs.50,000 ✓
194QPurchase of goods0.1%Rs.50 lakh

Thresholds above reflect Finance Act 2025 rationalisations effective FY 2025-26 onwards. Confirmed for FY 2026-27. ✓ Search every rate in the TDS Rate Finder.

PAN and non-filer higher rates

  • Section 206AA — if the payee does not furnish a PAN, deduct at the higher of the normal rate or 20%.
  • Section 206AB — higher TDS for specified non-filers of returns (where it applies for the period).
  • Always validate the payee's PAN and filing status before deducting at the normal rate.

The new Income-tax Act 2025 mapping

From FY 2026-27, the new Act renumbers TDS: salary to Section 392, non-salary payments consolidate into Section 393, and TCS to Section 394. The rates and thresholds broadly continue; the references change. See new TDS sections. Under the Income Tax Act, 2025 (effective 1 April 2026): TDS on payments → Section 393; TDS on salaries → Section 392; TCS → Section 394. ✓

Practical compliance

  • Deduct at the time of credit or payment, whichever is earlier.
  • Deposit by the 7th of the next month (30 April for March, in many cases).
  • File quarterly returns (24Q/26Q/27Q) and issue Form 16/16A.
  • Late deduction/deposit attracts interest under Section 201(1A).

Frequently asked questions

What is the TDS rate on professional fees for FY 2026-27?

10% under Section 194J for professional fees (2% for technical services and call-centre operators), once the annual threshold is crossed.

What happens if the payee has no PAN?

Under Section 206AA, TDS is deducted at the higher of the normal rate or 20% (and special rules apply to non-residents).

Have TDS sections changed under the new Act?

Yes. From FY 2026-27, salary TDS moves to Section 392, non-salary payments to Section 393, and TCS to Section 394, with rates broadly continuing.

Key takeaways

  • Common rates: 1-2% (194C), 10% (194J/194I), 2% (194H).
  • No PAN: deduct at higher of normal rate or 20% (206AA).
  • New Act renumbers TDS to Sections 392/393/394.
  • Deposit by the 7th; file 24Q/26Q/27Q; issue Form 16/16A.

posts.title: TDS return correction online (TRACES)
posts.excerpt: How to correct a filed TDS return online through TRACES - add or modify challans, correct PAN and deductee details, and resolve short-deduction or short-payment defaults.
posts.content:
A filed TDS return is corrected by filing a correction statement on the TRACES portal using the latest consolidated (conso) file. Corrections can add or modify challans, fix deductee PAN and amounts, and resolve short-deduction or short-payment defaults flagged in the justification report.

When a correction is needed

  • Wrong or invalid PAN of a deductee (causes the payee to lose credit).
  • Wrong challan details or unconsumed challan.
  • Short deduction / short payment / late-payment defaults.
  • Adding deductees or challans missed in the original return.

The process

  1. Download the conso file and the justification report from TRACES.
  2. Open it in the RPU/utility, make the corrections (PAN, challan, amount).
  3. Validate with the FVU and generate the correction file.
  4. Upload the correction statement (online on TRACES or at a TIN-FC).

Correction categories

TypeUsed for
Add challan / book entryAdd a missed challan
Update challanCorrect challan details
Update / add deducteeFix PAN, amount, section
PAN correctionCorrect an invalid deductee PAN

Practical points

  • Resolve defaults promptly to stop the payee's credit being blocked in 26AS.
  • Pay any short deduction/interest by challan before filing the correction.
  • There is no time limit bar for most online corrections, but act early.
  • See also fixing a 26AS mismatch.

Frequently asked questions

How do I correct a TDS return?

Download the conso file from TRACES, make the corrections in the utility, validate with the FVU, and upload a correction statement on TRACES.

Can I correct a wrong PAN in a filed TDS return?

Yes, through a PAN correction in the correction statement; this restores the deductee's TDS credit in Form 26AS.

Is there a deadline to file a TDS correction?

Most online corrections can be filed without a strict time bar, but it is best to correct promptly so the payee's credit is not delayed.

Key takeaways

  • Correct TDS returns via a correction statement on TRACES.
  • Use the conso file + justification report to identify defaults.
  • Fix PAN, challan and amount errors; pay shortfalls first.
  • Prompt correction restores the payee's 26AS credit.

faq.name: How do I correct a TDS return?

acceptedAnswer.text: Download the conso file from TRACES, make the corrections in the utility, validate with the FVU, and upload a correction statement on TRACES.

faq.name: Can I correct a wrong PAN in a filed TDS return?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, through a PAN correction in the correction statement; this restores the deductee's TDS credit in Form 26AS.

faq.name: Is there a deadline to file a TDS correction?

acceptedAnswer.text: Most online corrections can be filed without a strict time bar, but it is best to correct promptly so the payee's credit is not delayed.

posts.title: TDS return filing: 24Q, 26Q, 27Q
posts.excerpt: The TDS returns - Form 24Q for salary, 26Q for resident non-salary, 27Q for non-residents and 27EQ for TCS - their quarterly due dates and the late-fee under Section 234E.
posts.content:
TDS returns are filed quarterly: Form 24Q for salary, Form 26Q for resident non-salary payments, Form 27Q for payments to non-residents and Form 27EQ for TCS. Returns are due by 31 July, 31 October, 31 January and 31 May for the four quarters, with a late fee of Rs.200 per day under Section 234E.

Which form for which payment

FormCovers
24QTDS on salary (Section 192)
26QTDS on resident non-salary payments (194C, 194J, 194I, etc.)
27QTDS on payments to non-residents (Section 195)
27EQTCS (Section 206C)

Due dates

QuarterPeriodDue date
Q1Apr-Jun31 July
Q2Jul-Sep31 October
Q3Oct-Dec31 January
Q4Jan-Mar31 May

Late fee and penalty

  • Section 234E — late-filing fee of Rs.200 per day until the return is filed, capped at the TDS amount. This fee cannot be waived.
  • Section 271H — penalty of Rs.10,000 to Rs.1,00,000 for non-filing or incorrect filing.
  • Form 16 (salary) follows 24Q; Form 16A follows 26Q.

Practical points

  • Validate PANs before filing to avoid short-deduction defaults.
  • File even nil returns where applicable to avoid notices.
  • Correct errors through a correction statement.
  • Reconcile the return to challans and Form 26AS of payees.

Frequently asked questions

What is the difference between 24Q, 26Q and 27Q?

24Q is for salary TDS, 26Q for resident non-salary payments, 27Q for payments to non-residents, and 27EQ for TCS.

What are the TDS return due dates?

31 July, 31 October, 31 January and 31 May for quarters 1 to 4 respectively.

What is the late fee for a TDS return?

Rs.200 per day under Section 234E until filed (capped at the TDS amount), plus a possible penalty of Rs.10,000-1,00,000 under Section 271H.

Key takeaways

  • 24Q salary, 26Q resident non-salary, 27Q non-resident, 27EQ TCS.
  • Due 31 Jul / 31 Oct / 31 Jan / 31 May.
  • Section 234E late fee Rs.200/day (cannot be waived).
  • Issue Form 16 (24Q) and Form 16A (26Q) after filing.

acceptedAnswer.text: 24Q is for salary TDS, 26Q for resident non-salary payments, 27Q for payments to non-residents, and 27EQ for TCS.

faq.name: What are the TDS return due dates?

acceptedAnswer.text: 31 July, 31 October, 31 January and 31 May for quarters 1 to 4 respectively.

faq.name: What is the late fee for a TDS return?

acceptedAnswer.text: Rs.200 per day under Section 234E until filed (capped at the TDS amount), plus a possible penalty of Rs.10,000-1,00,000 under Section 271H.

posts.title: TDS on winnings: Section 194B and online gaming 194BA
posts.excerpt: TDS on winnings from lotteries and games (Section 194B) and online gaming (Section 194BA) is deducted at 30%. How net winnings are computed for online gaming and the no-threshold rule.
posts.content:
TDS on winnings from lotteries, crossword puzzles, card games and game shows is deducted at 30% under Section 194B, and winnings from online games are deducted at 30% under Section 194BA on net winnings. For online gaming there is no minimum threshold; winnings are taxed without any deduction or basic exemption.

Section 194B (lotteries, game shows)

Winnings from lotteries, crossword puzzles, card games, gambling, betting and game shows are subject to TDS at 30% under Section 194B where the amount exceeds Rs.10,000 (aggregate during the year). The income is taxed at a flat 30% under Section 115BB with no deductions, expenses or basic exemption.

Section 194BA (online gaming)

For online games, Section 194BA applies TDS at 30% on 'net winnings' in the user's account, computed at withdrawal and at year-end. There is no Rs.10,000 threshold — all net winnings are covered. Net winnings broadly = withdrawals + closing balance − deposits − opening balance, per the prescribed formula.

Comparison

Feature194B194BA
CoversLottery, game show, bettingOnline games
Rate30%30%
ThresholdRs.10,000None
BaseGross winningsNet winnings (formula)

Practical points

  • Winnings in kind require the payer to ensure tax is paid before release (grossed up).
  • No basic exemption, deduction or set-off against these winnings.
  • Report the income and TDS via 26AS/AIS.
  • The flat 30% (plus cess) means effective tax of about 31.2%.

Frequently asked questions

What is the TDS rate on lottery or game-show winnings?

30% under Section 194B where winnings exceed Rs.10,000; the income is taxed at a flat 30% with no deductions or basic exemption.

How is TDS on online gaming winnings calculated?

At 30% on net winnings under Section 194BA, computed at each withdrawal and at year-end using the prescribed formula; there is no minimum threshold.

Can I claim expenses against gaming winnings?

No. Winnings under Sections 194B/194BA are taxed at a flat 30% under Section 115BB with no deduction, expense or basic exemption allowed.

Key takeaways

  • 194B: 30% on lottery/game-show winnings above Rs.10,000.
  • 194BA: 30% on online-gaming net winnings, no threshold.
  • Flat 30% (about 31.2% with cess); no deductions or set-off.
  • Winnings in kind are grossed up before release.
